github.copilot.chat.commitMessageGenerat.text: Generate commit messages using Conventional Commits format: <type>[optional scope]: <description>
Classify the PRIMARY intent of the staged diff before writing the message.
Choose the most specific valid type, not the broadest one.
Use feat only when the staged changes introduce new functionality in production code or public developer-facing capabilities.
Use fix only when the staged changes correct broken, incorrect, unsafe, or unintended behavior in production code.
Use refactor only when production code is restructured without changing externally observable behavior, outputs, API contracts, persistence semantics, or business rules.
Use test only when the staged changes only add, remove, or modify tests, fixtures, mocks, test helpers, or test configuration, without changing production behavior.
Use docs only for documentation-only changes.
Use style only for formatting, whitespace, import ordering, or other non-semantic stylistic changes.
Use build only for package manager, dependency, compiler, bundler, or build system changes.
Use ci only for CI/CD workflows, pipelines, automation runners, or related scripts.
Use perf only when the primary purpose is measurable performance improvement.
Use chore only for maintenance work that does not fit build, ci, docs, style, test, perf, refactor, feat, or fix.
Use revert only when reverting a previous commit.
Never use feat for test-only changes.
Never use refactor when behavior changes, bug fixes, new features, schema changes, permission changes, or validation changes are introduced.
Never use chore when a more specific type fits.
Classify based on the actual staged diff, not filenames alone.
If both production code and tests are changed for the same logical purpose, classify by the production change, not by the presence of test files.
If the staged diff contains multiple unrelated logical changes, prefer separate commits instead of one combined message.
Include scope in parentheses when one module, bounded context, feature area, or package clearly dominates the staged diff.
Infer scope from the dominant module or component, for example auth, onboarding, billing, api, ui, db, infra, or tests.
Write the subject in imperative mood.
Keep the subject under 72 characters.
Commit message structure must follow this exact format:
<type>[optional scope]: <short description>
(blank line)
- bullet describing the main change
- bullet describing additional modification or context
- bullet describing impact, reason, or technical detail if applicable
Always insert exactly ONE blank line between the subject and the body section.
The body must never start immediately after the subject line.
If bullet points are present, the commit MUST contain the blank line separator.
When the staged diff contains more than one meaningful change, ALWAYS include a body section using hyphen bullet points.
Each bullet must summarize one concrete change detected in the diff.
Prefer 2 to 5 bullet points when a body is present.
Bullet lines must start with '- ' and stay under 100 characters.
A single-line commit message is allowed ONLY for trivial changes such as formatting, typo fixes, or single dependency updates.
Prefer lowercase type and scope.
Examples:
feat(auth): add magic-link login
(blank line)
- implement token-based login flow
- add API endpoint issuing login links
- extend session validation to support magic links
test(auth): add coverage for session expiration
(blank line)
- add tests for expired session redirect
- mock authentication state
- verify middleware rejects invalid sessions

github.copilot.chat.pullRequestDescripti.text: Always generate a structured pull request description using the following sections: Summary, Key Changes, Motivation, Testing, and Additional Notes.
Always format Key Changes as a bullet list with at least 3-6 specific items.
Always write in a professional, concise, engineering-focused tone. Avoid marketing language such as 'robust', 'seamless', 'enhanced', or 'improved'.
Always explain why the change was made, not just what changed.
Always include testing instructions describing how reviewers can verify the changes.
Never collapse the entire description into a single paragraph.
